prov.label: 125A
prov.heading: Requirement for annual personalised statement of contributions and accumulations for members

history.history-note: Section 125A: repealed, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Restrictions on transactions for scheme with fewer than 20 members

para.text: This section applies to a KiwiSaver scheme if the scheme has fewer than 20 members, treating all interests in the scheme held by persons associated under subpart YB of the Income Tax Act 2007 as being held by 1 person.
The provider must not lend money or provide financial assistance to—
a member:
a person associated (under subpart YB of the Income Tax Act 2007) with a provider or member.

history.history-note: Section 126: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Member’s interest in KiwiSaver scheme not assignable

para.text: Except as expressly provided in this Act, a member’s interest or any future benefits that will or may become payable to a member under the KiwiSaver scheme must not be assigned or charged or passed to any other person whether by way of security, operation of law, or any other means.

Subsection (1) does not prevent a member’s interest or any future benefits that will or may become payable to a member under the KiwiSaver scheme from being released, assigned, or charged, or from passing to any other person if it is required by the provisions of any enactment, including a requirement by order of the court under any enactment (including an order made under section 31 of the Property (Relationships) Act 1976).

Subsection (1) also does not prevent an amount up to the value of the member’s accumulation from being released to the Official Assignee of New Zealand on behalf of the Crown if that release is required because of a civil forfeiture order within the meaning of section 5(1) of the Criminal Proceeds (Recovery) Act 2009.

history.history-note: Section 127: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Section 127(2): amended, on 1 April 2023, by section 46(1) of the Criminal Proceeds (Recovery) Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 6).
Section 127(3): inserted, on 1 April 2023, by section 46(2) of the Criminal Proceeds (Recovery) Amendment Act 2023 (2023 No 6).

prov.heading: Application of Financial Transactions Reporting Act 1996 to default allocation of members to KiwiSaver schemes

para.text: This section applies in respect of any allocation of a person (A) to, or any application by a person (A) to become a member of, a KiwiSaver scheme under sections 50 to 52.

For the purposes of section 6 of the Financial Transactions Reporting Act 1996, an application or allocation to which this section applies is not a request to a financial institution for the person to become a facility holder as defined in section 2(1) of that Act.
Despite subsection (2),—
a provider of a KiwiSaver scheme must make reasonable efforts to verify A’s identity at the time that A becomes a member of a KiwiSaver scheme; and

if A makes a voluntary payment into the KiwiSaver scheme, the payment must, for the purposes of section 6 of the Financial Transactions Reporting Act 1996, unless the provider has already verified A’s identity, be treated as a request to a financial institution for the person to become a facility holder within the meaning of that Act.
In this section, voluntary payment means,—

in relation to a member of a KiwiSaver scheme who has become a member of that scheme under section 48 or 52, a payment made by or for the benefit of that person into the KiwiSaver scheme that is not a deduction from salary or wages made under subpart 1 of Part 3; and

in relation to a member of a KiwiSaver scheme to whom paragraph (a) does not apply, a payment made by or for the benefit of that person into the KiwiSaver scheme that is in excess of the amount the member is contractually bound to pay into the scheme within a defined period.

prov.heading: Product disclosure statements must contain responsible investment statement

para.text: Every product disclosure statement relating to an offer of interests in a KiwiSaver scheme or a complying superannuation fund must contain a statement in the following form if it is a scheme that takes responsible investment, including environmental, social, and governance considerations, into account in the investment policies and procedures of the scheme:

Responsible investment, including environmental, social, and governance considerations, is taken into account in the investment policies and procedures of the scheme as at the date of this product disclosure statement. You can obtain an explanation of the extent to which responsible investment is taken into account in those policies and procedures at the issuer's Internet site at [specify Internet site address].

Every product disclosure statement relating to an offer of interests in a KiwiSaver scheme or a complying superannuation fund must contain a statement in the following form if it is a scheme that does not take responsible investment, including environmental, social, and governance considerations, into account in the investment policies and procedures of the scheme:

Responsible investment, including environmental, social, and governance considerations, is not taken into account in the investment policies and procedures of the scheme as at the date of this product disclosure statement.

A failure to comply with this section must be treated as if it were a failure to comply with the requirements of regulations made under the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 as to the information that a product disclosure statement must contain.

history.history-note: Section 129: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Implied provision as to alterations to scheme

prov.heading: Implied provision as to reversion of scheme assets to employer

history.history-note: Section 129B: repealed, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Heading: inserted, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Application of Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013

para.text: An employer does not contravene the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 by reason only that, acting as an employer, that person—
complies with the person’s responsibilities as an employer under this Act; or
chooses a KiwiSaver scheme as the employer’s chosen KiwiSaver scheme under section 47.

No act or omission by the Crown, or any officer or employee of the Crown, that occurs during the performance or exercise, or intended performance or exercise, of any functions, duties, or powers in respect of KiwiSaver schemes and complying superannuation funds, gives rise to any civil or criminal liability of the Crown or those officers or employees under the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013.

history.history-note: Section 130: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Certain sections of Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 modified in relation to KiwiSaver scheme

para.text: The manager of a KiwiSaver scheme must provide the Commissioner with notice of—
any member—
who has given notice under section 35(2) or 54(2) of the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013; or
to whom money would have had to have been repaid under section 80(1)﻿(a) or 85(3)﻿(a) of that Act, but for subsection (2) of this section; or
who has not confirmed that he or she still wants to acquire the financial products under section 80(2) or 85(4) of that Act; and

if all or part of the consideration for the issue of financial products to that member was the transfer of the member’s accumulation from another KiwiSaver scheme, the name of that scheme from which the member’s accumulation was transferred; and
the name, address, and tax file number of the member.
If a member is notified to the Commissioner under subsection (1),—
the following do not apply:
any right of the member under the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 to withdraw from holding the financial product and to have the relevant money repaid; and
any duty of the issuer or any other person to repay any contribution under section 36, 55, 80, or 85 of the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013; and
instead, the following both apply:
a process for the person to be allocated to a new scheme (see sections 50 to 52 and 211); and
a process for the person to be transferred to a new scheme (see section 57).

history.history-note: Section 131: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Subpart 2: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Appointment of default providers

para.text: The Minister may appoint 1 or more managers for a specified term to provide—
a default KiwiSaver scheme that is specified in the instrument of appointment; and
a default investment product of that default KiwiSaver scheme that is specified in the instrument of appointment.
The appointment may be made subject to such terms and conditions as the Minister considers fit.
The instrument of appointment must—
identify the default KiwiSaver scheme and the default investment product of the scheme:
state any terms and conditions of the appointment:
state any prescribed information.
In determining whether to appoint a manager as a default KiwiSaver provider under this section, the Minister must seek the advice of the FMA.
A restricted scheme is not eligible to be a default KiwiSaver scheme.

history.history-note: Section 132: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Provisions of instrument of appointment to prevail over provisions of trust deed

para.text: The manager and the supervisor must take all reasonable steps to ensure that the trust deed of a default KiwiSaver scheme is consistent with the instrument of appointment.
The provisions of the instrument of appointment prevail over the terms of the trust deed establishing the KiwiSaver scheme that relate to the KiwiSaver scheme.

A product disclosure statement relating to an offer of interests in a KiwiSaver scheme to which an instrument of appointment under section 132 relates must draw attention to the implications of this section.

history.history-note: Section 133: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Effect of appointment under section 132

para.text: If a person is appointed as a provider of a default KiwiSaver scheme under an instrument of appointment under section 132,—
the default KiwiSaver scheme must be shown as a default KiwiSaver scheme on the register of managed investment schemes; and
the Commissioner may nominate the default investment product of the scheme as a default investment product to which persons may be allocated for the purposes of sections 50 to 52.

history.history-note: Section 134: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Appointment must be notified

para.text: The Minister must, as soon as practicable after an appointment under section 132 has been made, notify the FMA, the Commissioner, and the Registrar of Financial Service Providers (for the purposes of making the appointment available and updating the register of managed investment schemes) that the appointment has been made, and provide each of them with a copy of the instrument of appointment.
This section also applies to any variation, renewal, or revocation of the instrument of appointment.

history.history-note: Section 135: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Power of High Court to act in respect of terms and conditions of appointment as default KiwiSaver scheme and regulations relating to default KiwiSaver schemes

para.text: This section applies if the High Court is satisfied that the manager of a default KiwiSaver scheme appointed under section 132 intends to engage, or is engaging or has engaged, in conduct that constitutes, or would constitute,—
a breach of the terms and conditions of the instrument of appointment referred to in section 132; or
a breach of regulations made under section 230.

Sections 480 and 481 of the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 (which relate to injunctions) apply as if that conduct or intended conduct were a contravention of that Act or regulations made under that Act.
Without limiting those sections, the High Court may make any orders on any terms and conditions that it thinks appropriate, including—
an order to restrain the manager or the supervisor of the scheme, or both, from engaging in conduct that constitutes, or would constitute, the breach:
an order to require the manager or the supervisor of the scheme, or both,—
to do a particular act or thing:
to comply with the conditions of the instrument of appointment:
an interim order.

history.history-note: Section 136: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Revocations, etc, of instruments of appointment

para.text: An instrument of appointment may provide for its renewal or variation or expiry or revocation by the Minister or the provider.
Despite any matter provided for in an instrument of appointment as to its expiry or revocation, the Minister may, by notice in writing to a provider, revoke an instrument of appointment if—
the FMA directs that a scheme's registration as a KiwiSaver scheme be removed under section 134 of the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013; or
the FMA directs that a scheme's registration as a managed investment scheme be cancelled under section 195 of that Act; or
an order or resolution to wind up the scheme is made as referred to in section 212 of that Act; or
the Minister is satisfied that—
the provider is not operating in accordance with the terms and conditions of the instrument of appointment; and
the failure to operate in accordance with the terms and conditions of the instrument of appointment is a significant breach as prescribed in regulations made under section 230.
The appointment of the provider under section 132 ceases on revocation of the instrument of appointment.

The Minister must notify the FMA, the Commissioner, and the Registrar of Financial Service Providers (for the purpose of updating the register of managed investment schemes) as soon as practicable after an instrument of appointment is revoked.

history.history-note: Section 137: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Duration of obligations as default provider after terminating event

para.text: In this section,—
reporting obligations, in relation to a provider and the provider’s scheme, means—
any requirement for the provider to report to persons specified in an instrument of appointment; and

any requirement for the provider to produce to any persons specified in an instrument of appointment any papers, documents, records, or things in respect of the scheme (and the power of any person to require production of those papers, documents, records, or things)
terminating event means—
the revocation of an instrument of appointment by the provider or the Minister under the terms and conditions of the instrument of appointment; or
the revocation of an instrument of appointment in accordance with section 137(2); or
the expiry of the term of appointment (as specified in the instrument of appointment and in accordance with any renewal of the term of appointment).
Despite any terminating event,—

any terms and conditions of the instrument of appointment that relate to a provider’s reporting obligations in respect of the provider’s scheme continue to apply until the date when the term of appointment would have expired but for the terminating event; and

regulations made under section 230 continue to apply in relation to the provider until the provider has completed every act or thing that the regulations require the provider to do following any terminating event.

history.history-note: Section 138: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Subpart 3: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Outline of subpart

para.text: This subpart contains rules relating to complying superannuation funds (which are schemes that were approved for the purposes of the Income Tax Act 2007 under section 35 of the Superannuation Schemes Act 1989 before its repeal) as follows:
the rules that apply to complying superannuation funds:
the rules relating to revocation of approval of complying superannuation funds.
These rules are in addition to rules elsewhere in this Act that apply to complying superannuation funds (for example, in relation to implied terms and unreasonable fees).

history.history-note: Section 139: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Failure to pay: provider notice

para.text: This section applies if the provider of a complying superannuation fund knows that an employer has failed to pay to the provider an amount of compulsory employer contribution in accordance with subpart 3A of Part 3.

The provider must, as soon as practicable, give a notice to the employer requesting the payment of the amount of compulsory employer contribution. The provider must send to the FMA a copy of the notice.

If the employer does not pay the amount of compulsory employer contribution to the provider within 1 month of this section first applying for the amount, and the total of the amounts of compulsory employer contributions unpaid is more than $500, then the provider must immediately give a notice to the FMA.
A notice under subsection (3) must specify the following:
the name of the employer; and
the amounts of compulsory employer contributions unpaid; and
the employer’s name, address, and tax file number (if known); and
the relevant employees to whom the failure to pay relates and their tax file numbers and addresses; and
the pay periods and relevant amounts for the employees to whom the failure to pay relates; and
other information required by the FMA.

If the employer pays an amount of compulsory employer contribution remedying a failure to pay that was notified to the FMA under subsection (3), the provider must immediately give a notice to the FMA showing relevant details of the employer’s payment.

history.history-note: Section 140: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Failure to pay: FMA's duties

para.text: If the FMA receives a notice under section 140(3), the FMA must decide the amount of compulsory employer contribution that an employer to which the notice relates has failed to pay for the relevant calendar months.

The FMA may use any power (with necessary modifications for complying superannuation funds) that the FMA has in respect of KiwiSaver schemes in the performance of the duty to decide imposed by subsection (1).
As soon as practicable, the FMA must give a notice to the employer showing the information described in subsection (4).
A notice under subsection (3) must—
require the payment of the amount (the liable amount) that the FMA has decided, under subsection (1), that an employer has failed to pay to the provider; and
specify the relevant calendar months and related amounts; and
specify that the employer must pay the liable amount within 28 days after the notice is given; and
specify the employer's name, address, and tax file number (if known); and
specify the relevant employees to whom the failure to pay relates and their tax file numbers and addresses; and
specify the pay periods and relevant amounts for the employees to whom the failure to pay relates; and
inform the employer that failure to comply with the notice will result in the Commissioner receiving notice of the failure to comply; and
show other information required by the Commissioner.

If the employer does not pay the liable amount in the period specified in subsection (4)﻿(c) and the employer has not appealed against the FMA's decision under subsection (1) within the time allowed under section 156, the FMA must immediately—
give to the Commissioner a notice showing the information described in subsection (6); and
send to the provider a copy of the notice.
A notice under subsection (5) must—
state that the employer has failed to comply with notices under section 140(2) and subsection (3) of this section; and
show the information described in subsection (4); and
specify the extent to which an amount of compulsory employer contributions remains unpaid for the liable amount; and
specify the relevant employees to whom the unpaid amounts relate and their tax file numbers and addresses; and
specify the pay periods and relevant amounts for the employees to whom the unpaid amounts relate.

history.history-note: Section 141: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Failure to pay: Commissioner

para.text: If the Commissioner receives a notice under section 141(5), the amount of compulsory employer contributions unpaid for the liable amount, specified in that notice, is treated as an amount due and payable by the employer to the Commissioner on the 20th working day after the Commissioner receives the notice under section 141(5).
The Commissioner must send the employer a notice of the amount due and payable, and the due date, specified in subsection (1).

history.history-note: Section 142: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Recovered amounts

para.text: An amount of compulsory employer contribution for an employee's complying superannuation fund that is received by the FMA or the Commissioner by virtue of subpart 3A of Part 3 or this subpart must be paid by them to the relevant provider. The relevant amount of compulsory employer contributions remaining unpaid for the relevant liable amount is consequentially reduced.

history.history-note: Section 143: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Reduction of scheme insurance upon transfer out of complying superannuation fund

para.text: This section applies to a complying superannuation fund (scheme A) if—
scheme A provides or facilitates the provision of insurance (the scheme insurance) to a member or other beneficiary (the person); and
the benefit of the scheme insurance is calculated by reference to contributions for the person held by a complying superannuation fund (the contributions); and
an amount of contributions is transferred out of the complying superannuation fund to a complying superannuation fund or KiwiSaver scheme (other than scheme A).

A term is implied into the trust deed of scheme A. That term must have the effect of allowing the benefit of the person's scheme insurance to be reduced in proportion to the amount of contributions transferred out of the complying superannuation fund to a complying superannuation fund or KiwiSaver scheme (other than scheme A).

history.history-note: Section 144: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Revocation of approval of complying superannuation funds

para.text: The FMA may revoke the approval of a scheme as a complying superannuation fund if—
the FMA is satisfied that the scheme no longer meets the requirements in subsection (2); or
the manager of the scheme makes an application for revocation.
The requirements are that—

the complying superannuation fund section of the scheme and any relevant participation agreement must contain rules that subject the following to complying fund rules (as defined in section YA 1 of the Income Tax Act 2007):
relevant contributions:
returns on relevant contributions:
relevant benefits; and

the scheme must have at least 20 members, treating all interests in the scheme or account held by associated persons within the meaning of section YA 1 of the Income Tax Act 2007 as being held by 1 person; and
the fund must have rules that meet all the requirements set out in Schedule 28 of the Income Tax Act 2007 (requirements for complying fund rules) and do not detract from those requirements.
The FMA must not exercise a power under subsection (1)﻿(a), or refuse an application for a revocation made by a manager of a scheme, unless—
the FMA gives the manager of the scheme no less than 10 working days' written notice of the following matters before it exercises the power:
that the FMA may exercise the power or refuse the application (as the case may be); and
the reasons why it may do so; and
the FMA gives the manager or the manager's representative an opportunity to make written submissions on the matter within that notice period.
The FMA must, as soon as practicable after revoking the approval, give notice to—
the manager of the scheme; and
the Registrar of Financial Service Providers, for the purpose of updating the register of managed investment schemes; and
any person who originally applied for approval under section 34 of the Superannuation Schemes Act 1989; and
the Commissioner.
The manager of a scheme who is notified that approval is revoked must immediately—
give notice of that revocation to each member who may be affected, and to their employers; and
give notice to the Commissioner of each member who may be affected, and of their employers.
Each of those notices must specify an effective revocation date.

history.history-note: Section 145: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Subpart 4: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Effect of being exempt employer and outline of subpart

para.text: A person who starts new employment with an exempt employer is exempt from the automatic enrolment rules.
For the avoidance of doubt, subsection (1)—
does not prevent an employee of an exempt employer from opting in under subpart 1 of Part 2; and

does not prevent a person who is already a member of a KiwiSaver scheme from becoming liable for automatic deduction of contributions from the salary or wages paid in respect of employment with an exempt employer under section 15(1)﻿(a)﻿(ii) or 36(1)﻿(a)﻿(ii).
This subpart contains rules relating to exempt employers (as approved under section 30 before its repeal by the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013) as follows:
the rules that apply to exempt employers:
the rules relating to revocation of approval of exempt employers.
These rules are in addition to rules elsewhere in this Act that apply to exempt employers.

history.history-note: Section 146: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Eligibility to continue to be exempt employer

para.text: An employer is eligible to continue to be an exempt employer if the FMA is satisfied that the employer provides access to a superannuation scheme or workplace savings scheme for its employees that complies with the following rules:

every person who becomes a permanent employee (including a part-time employee) of that employer, and who is aged 18 or over but less than the New Zealand superannuation qualification age, must be eligible, in practice, at the time when the person so becomes an employee,—
to become a member of the scheme; and
to transfer to the scheme the member’s accumulation in relation to other superannuation schemes or workplace savings schemes (to the extent that transfers are available from those other schemes); and

the trust deed of the scheme must have the effect that each member who satisfies the scheme’s requirements for a withdrawal benefit, and who elects to withdraw from membership of the scheme, may transfer the member’s accumulation to another superannuation scheme, workplace savings scheme, or KiwiSaver scheme (to the extent that transfers are available to those other schemes); and

the trust deed of the scheme must provide for an amount equal to at least 4% of annual gross base salary or wages to be contributed to, or otherwise credited within, the scheme in respect of each person who becomes a permanent employee of that employer and a member of the scheme.
However, subsection (1)﻿(c) does not apply—
to the extent that an employee is, in accordance with the terms of the scheme, temporarily relieved from contributions at that rate (for example, in the event of financial hardship); or

if the scheme is a defined benefit scheme of a type that does not satisfy the 4% minimum amount rule in subsection (1)﻿(c), and if the actuary of the scheme certifies, to the satisfaction of the FMA, that the value of each employee’s accrued benefits to be provided by the scheme is, as a matter of fact, increasing, during each membership period, by an amount at least equivalent to such minimum amount that would otherwise be required by this section and section 148.
In this section,—
defined benefit scheme has the same meaning as in section 6(1) of the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013
permanent employees means employees—
who are not employed in temporary employment (as described in section 12); and
to whom the automatic enrolment rules would apply, but for the application of this section.

history.history-note: Section 147: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: How 4% minimum amount may be calculated for exempt employer defined contribution schemes

para.text: For the purposes of section 147(1)﻿(c),—
the minimum amount required by section 147(1)﻿(c) may be made up—
entirely of contributions by the employee; or
entirely of contributions by an employer; or
partially of contributions by the employee and partially of contributions by an employer; and
the minimum amount required by section 147(1)﻿(c) must be treated as satisfied if the sum of the following amounts is equal to at least 4% of annual gross base salary or wages:
the minimum prescribed amount that the employee must contribute:
the maximum prescribed amount that the employer would be required to contribute if the member were to contribute the maximum prescribed amount:
any amount contributed to the scheme by an employer in respect of an employee does not count towards the minimum amount required by section 147(1)﻿(c) unless—
the employee is legally entitled to require the employer to contribute that amount on his or her behalf; and

the trust deed of the scheme provides for the minimum amount required by section 147(1)﻿(c) to vest completely in the employee no later than the time when the employee begins his or her sixth year as a member of the scheme; and

any amount contributed to the scheme by an employer in respect of an employee must be calculated, for the purposes of the minimum amount required by section 147(1)﻿(c), before any ESCT payable under the ESCT rules is deducted.
Subsection (1)﻿(b) does not limit subsection (1)﻿(c) or (d).

Company A provides access to a superannuation scheme for its employees. The trust deed provides that employees, if they decide to become members, must contribute at either 1% or 3% of annual gross base salary. The employer is obliged to match the employee’s contributions (eg, if employee contributes 1%, the company must contribute 1%).
The scheme complies with the rule as to the 4% minimum contribution as follows:

legtable.summary: The following table is small in size and contains 2 columns without headings.

para.text: Minimum amount employee member must contribute
Maximum amount that employer must contribute in respect of employee member

history.history-note: Section 148: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Exempt employers who provide access to more than 1 scheme

para.text: An employer who provides access to more than 1 superannuation scheme or workplace savings scheme for its employees is eligible to continue to be an exempt employer if the FMA is satisfied that, if all of those schemes were considered as a whole (as if they were 1 scheme), the rules in section 147 would be complied with.

history.history-note: Section 149: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Exempt employers who have schemes established under master trusts

para.text: An employer who provides access to a superannuation scheme or workplace savings scheme for its employees that is established under a master trust is eligible to continue to be an exempt employer if the FMA is satisfied that the rules in section 147 would be complied with if the FMA considered only—
the master trust in so far as it relates to the employer’s scheme; and
the participation agreement executed by the employer in relation to the membership of the employer’s employees in the scheme; and
anything else that the FMA decides is relevant to evidencing compliance with the rules in section 147, in respect of the employer’s employees.

history.history-note: Section 150: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Employers may apply to succeed to former employer's exempt employer status

para.text: A person may make an application to the FMA for approval to succeed to an exempt employer's exempt employer status if the person is a succeeding employer for that exempt employer.
The application must be accompanied by—
information that satisfies the FMA that the rules in section 147 (or, if applicable, section 149 or 150) are complied with; and
the names, addresses, and tax file numbers of each employer in respect of whom the application is made; and

if the application is made in respect of an employer that is part of a group of companies, any details of the names, addresses, tax file numbers, and payroll arrangements of any other members of the group that the FMA may request.
In this section, succeeding employer means an employer who succeeds an exempt employer because of a merger with, or an acquisition of, the exempt employer.

history.history-note: Section 151: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: How applications to succeed to former employer's exempt employer status must be dealt with

para.text: The FMA must, within 20 working days after receiving an application made under section 151(1) and the documents required under section 151(2) to accompany the application,—
consider whether the FMA is satisfied that each employer in respect of whom the application is made complies with the rules in section 147 or, if applicable, section 149 or 150; and
if so satisfied, approve the employer as an exempt employer and register the employer on the register of exempt employers kept under section 155.
The FMA must—
give notice to the employer as soon as practicable after approving, or declining to approve, the employer as an exempt employer; and

specify in that notice an effective date after which an employee who starts new employment with the employer will be exempt from the automatic enrolment rules (unless those rules do not otherwise apply).

history.history-note: Section 152: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Revocation of exempt employer approval

para.text: The FMA may revoke an approval as an exempt employer given under this Act if—

the FMA is satisfied that the employer is no longer eligible to be an exempt employer because the employer no longer provides access to a scheme for its employees that complies with the rules in section 147 or, if applicable, section 149 or 150; or
the employer makes an application for revocation.
The FMA must not exercise a power under subsection (1)﻿(a), or refuse an application for a revocation made by an employer, unless—
the FMA gives the employer no less than 10 working days' written notice of the following matters before it exercises the power:
that the FMA may exercise the power or refuse the application (as the case may be); and
the reasons why it may do so; and
the FMA gives the employer or the employer's representative an opportunity to make written submissions on the matter within that notice period.
The FMA must—
give notice to the employer as soon as practicable after revoking the approval; and

specify in that notice an effective revocation date after which an employee who starts new employment with the employer will be subject to the automatic enrolment rules (unless those rules do not otherwise apply); and
ensure that the employer is removed from the register of exempt employers kept under section 155.

history.history-note: Section 153: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: FMA must give notice to Commissioner of exempt employers

para.text: The FMA must give notice to the Commissioner as soon as practicable after an employer is approved under section 152 or an approval is revoked under section 153.

history.history-note: Section 154: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Register of exempt employers

para.text: The FMA must ensure that a register is kept by the FMA or under subsection (2) that contains the names of employers who are exempt employers.
That register may be part of the register of managed investment schemes, in which case the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 applies to the keeping of the register accordingly.

history.history-note: Section 155: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Subpart 5: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Right of appeal against certain decisions of FMA

para.text: A person affected by a decision of the FMA under any of the following provisions may appeal against the decision to the High Court:
section 141 (failure to pay: FMA's duties):
section 145 (revocation of approval of complying superannuation funds):
section 152 (how applications to succeed to former employer's exempt employer status must be dealt with):
section 153 (revocation of exempt employer approval).
A decision against which an appeal is lodged under this section continues in force unless the High Court orders otherwise.

history.history-note: Section 156: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Sharing of information and documents with Commissioner for purpose of administering KiwiSaver schemes

para.text: The FMA may provide to the Commissioner any information, or a copy of any document, that the FMA—

holds in relation to the performance or exercise of the FMA’s functions, powers, or duties under this Act or in connection with 1 or more KiwiSaver schemes or complying superannuation funds under this Act or any other enactment; and

considers may assist the Commissioner in the performance or exercise of the Commissioner's functions, powers, or duties under this Act or in connection with 1 or more KiwiSaver schemes or complying superannuation funds under this Act or any other enactment.

The FMA may use any information, or a copy of any document, provided to it by the Commissioner under any enactment in the FMA’s performance or exercise of its functions, powers, or duties under this Act or in connection with 1 or more KiwiSaver schemes or complying superannuation funds under this Act or any other enactment.
This section applies despite anything to the contrary in any contract, deed, or document.

history.history-note: Section 157: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Conditions that may be imposed on providing information, documents, or evidence to Commissioner

para.text: The FMA may impose any conditions in relation to providing information or documents to the Commissioner (whether in compliance with a request or otherwise).
The FMA must, in considering what conditions to impose, have regard to whether conditions are necessary or desirable in order to protect the privacy of any individual.
Those conditions may include, without limitation, conditions relating to—
maintaining the confidentiality of anything provided (in particular, information that is personal information within the meaning of the Privacy Act 2020):
the storing of, use of, or access to anything provided:
the copying, returning, or disposing of copies of documents provided.

history.history-note: Section 158: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Section 158(3)﻿(a): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Annual report by FMA

para.text: The FMA must, within 3 months after the end of every financial year, report to the Minister on the principal matters transacted under this Act during that year.
Every report must be presented to the House of Representatives by the Minister as soon as practicable after it has been received by that Minister.

prov.cf: 1989 No 10 s 28; 2006 No 40 s 194 as at October 2011

history.history-note: Section 159: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Offence to supply false or misleading information, etc

para.text: Every person commits an offence, and is liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding the amount set out in subsection (3), who makes any statement or supplies any paper, document, record, report, copy, thing, or certificate required by this Act knowing that it is false or misleading.
If a body corporate commits an offence under this section, every officer of the body corporate who knowingly authorises or permits the offence also commits the offence.
A person who is convicted of an offence under this section is liable to a fine not exceeding $300,000.

history.history-note: Section 160: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: No Crown guarantee of KiwiSaver schemes or products

para.text: There is no Crown guarantee in respect of any KiwiSaver scheme or investment product of a KiwiSaver scheme.
Every product disclosure statement relating to an offer of interests in a KiwiSaver scheme must contain a statement to that effect.

history.history-note: Section 161: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 92 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Factual description of, or transmission of information about, KiwiSaver scheme not financial advice service

para.text: For the avoidance of doubt, the Crown or any other person does not provide a financial advice service for the purposes of the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013 if the Crown or that person—
supplies an information pack as required or authorised by this Act; or
gives a factual description to another person of the features of a KiwiSaver scheme or of KiwiSaver schemes (for example, information about admission as a member or termination of membership); or
gives information of the type referred to in paragraph (b) in the course of promoting the benefits of retirement savings in general; or
acts only as an intermediary who transmits information about a KiwiSaver scheme; or
otherwise exercises or carries out a function, duty, or power under this Act.

prov.heading: Unique identifiers

para.text: In this section, specified functions, duties, or powers means functions, duties, or powers that—
a provider is required to exercise or carry out for the purposes of this Act; or
are reasonably necessary for the effective administration of the overall KiwiSaver scheme.
Despite information privacy principle 13(2) or (5) set out in section 22 of the Privacy Act 2020, a provider of a KiwiSaver scheme may—
require an individual to disclose any unique identifier that has been assigned to that person by the Commissioner for the purposes of carrying out specified functions, duties, or powers; and
assign to a proposed member, or member, of the KiwiSaver scheme any unique identifier assigned to that person by the Commissioner.

This section does not authorise a provider of a KiwiSaver scheme to use a unique identifier assigned by the Commissioner, except for the purpose of carrying out the specified functions, duties, or powers.

history.history-note: Section 207(2): amended, on 1 December 2020, by section 217 of the Privacy Act 2020 (2020 No 31).

prov.heading: Information held by Commissioner in respect of person who has opted out or who should not have been allocated to overall KiwiSaver scheme

para.text: This section applies—
if an employee has given an opt-out notice to the Commissioner or to an employer under section 17 and the relevant date referred to in subsection (2) has expired; or

a person is allocated to a KiwiSaver scheme in circumstances in which this Act does not require the person to be allocated to a KiwiSaver scheme and the person does not opt into any KiwiSaver scheme or choose to remain in the KiwiSaver scheme to which the person is allocated.
For the purposes of subsection (1)﻿(a), the relevant date is,—

in a case in which the opt-out notice is given to the Commissioner and it does not result in a requirement under this Act for contributions that are in the possession of the Commissioner to be repaid, the date the opt-out notice is accepted by the Commissioner; or

in a case in which the opt-out notice is received by an employer and it does not result in a requirement under this Act for contributions that are in the possession of the Commissioner to be repaid, the date the Commissioner receives a copy of the notice from the employer under section 17(5); or

in a case in which the opt-out notice results in a requirement under this Act for contributions that are in the possession of the Commissioner to be repaid, the date of the final refund of those contributions.

The Commissioner must not use personal information collected in respect of a person to whom subsection (1) applies for purposes other than the administration of the KiwiSaver scheme if the personal information—
was provided to the Commissioner in respect of a member as required by this Act or for the purposes of this Act; and
has not been used by the Commissioner for purposes related to any of the other Inland Revenue Acts.

history.history-note: Heading: repealed, on 1 December 2014, by section 94 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Application of Securities Act 1978

history.history-note: Section 209: repealed, on 1 December 2014, by section 94 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Certain sections of Securities Act 1978 modified in relation to KiwiSaver scheme

history.history-note: Section 210: repealed, on 1 December 2014, by section 94 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Duty of Commissioner under section 50 modified in certain cases in which section 131 applies

para.text: This section applies if—
the Commissioner receives a notice under section 131(1); and
the notice states the name of that scheme from which the member’s accumulation was transferred (the member’s previous KiwiSaver scheme).

If this section applies, the Commissioner must apply section 50 as if a reference to a default investment product of a default KiwiSaver scheme were instead a reference to the investment product or products of the member’s previous KiwiSaver scheme to which the member’s accumulation was applied.
Subsection (2) does not apply if, in the opinion of the Commissioner, the application of section 50 in accordance with that subsection is not practicable.

history.history-note: Section 211 heading: amended, on 1 December 2014, by section 95(1) of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Section 211(1)﻿(a): amended, on 1 December 2014, by section 95(2) of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Section 211(1)﻿(b): amended (with effect on 1 July 2007), on 19 December 2007, by section 90(1) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 211(2): amended (with effect on 1 July 2007), on 19 December 2007, by section 90(2) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).

prov.heading: Persons may request reconsideration of certain decisions of Commissioner

para.text: This section applies to any matter that under Parts 2 and 3 is left to the discretion, judgment, opinion, approval, consent, or determination of the Commissioner.

A person affected by the Commissioner’s decision on the matter has 20 working days from the date on which notice of the decision was given to the person (or any longer period allowed by the Commissioner) to—
request the Commissioner to reconsider the decision; and
give the Commissioner any information in support of that request.
The Commissioner may require a person to make that request by notice.
On receiving a request, the Commissioner may—
accept the person’s request; or
seek further information from the person; or
decline the person’s request; or
accept or decline the person’s request in part and decline or accept the other part.

prov.heading: Reconsideration of other decisions

para.text: Part 8A of the Tax Administration Act 1994 applies to every notice of a disputable decision given by the Commissioner under this Act.

prov.heading: Application of section 215

para.text: Section 215 set out penalties that apply, in addition to the provisions of the Tax Administration Act 1994, if there is a failure to provide information under Part 2 or 3.

history.history-note: Section 214 heading: amended, on 1 April 2009, by section 91(1) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 214(1): amended, on 1 April 2009, by section 91(2)﻿(a) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 214(1): amended, on 1 April 2009, by section 91(2)﻿(b) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 214(2): repealed, on 1 April 2009, by section 91(3) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).

prov.heading: Penalty for employer to fail to provide information

para.text: Every employer is liable for the penalty in subsection (2) who fails to give information as required by Part 2 or 3.
The penalty is—
nil if the Commissioner has not given notice to the employer, within the preceding 12 months, that—
a penalty may be imposed on the employer if the employer does not provide information as required by Part 2 or 3:
the employer has been liable under subsection (1) in the preceding 12 months; and
in any other case,—
$50 if the employer is a small employer; and
$250 if the employer is not a small employer.
However, an employer is not liable for more than 1 penalty per month.
In this section, a person is a small employer—

if the person was an employer in the preceding tax year in respect of whom gross tax deductions or withholdings payable and ESCT payable in that preceding tax year were, in total, less than $100,000; or
if the person was not an employer in the preceding year, until the time when gross tax deductions payable and ESCT payable in the current tax year, in total, exceed $100,000.

The penalty is due and payable on the day on which the next tax deduction required to be made by the employer under the PAYE rules is due to be paid to the Commissioner after the end of the PAYE period in which the failure occurred.

history.history-note: Section 215(2)﻿(a): substituted (with effect on 1 July 2007), on 19 December 2007, by section 92(1) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 215(3): amended, on 19 December 2007, by section 92(2) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 215(4): amended, on 1 April 2009, by section 92(3) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 215(4)﻿(a): amended, on 1 April 2008, by section ZA 2(1) of the Income Tax Act 2007 (2007 No 97).
Section 215(4)﻿(b): amended, on 1 April 2008, by section ZA 2(1) of the Income Tax Act 2007 (2007 No 97).

prov.heading: Penalty for employer to fail or incorrectly make deductions, or to short pay compulsory employer contributions

history.history-note: Section 216: repealed, on 1 April 2009, by section 94 of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).

prov.heading: Giving of notices by and to Commissioner

para.text: Sections 14 to 14G of the Tax Administration Act 1994 apply when this Act requires either the Commissioner to give a notice to a person, or a person to give a notice to the Commissioner.

history.history-note: Section 217: replaced, on 2 June 2016, by section 217 of the Taxation (Transformation: First Phase Simplification and Other Measures) Act 2016 (2016 No 27).

prov.heading: Giving of notices to other persons

para.text: This section applies when this Act requires a person to give a notice to a person other than the Commissioner.
The person must give the notice in writing.
The person may use the methods set out in subsections (4) to (7) to give the notice, subject to any conditions described in those subsections.
The person may give the notice by personal delivery to an addressee who is not a corporate body.
The person may give the notice by personal delivery to an addressee that is a corporate body, if the personal delivery is made to the addressee’s office during working hours.
The person may give the notice by an electronic means of communication to the addressee, if the person complies with Part 4 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017.
The person may give the notice by posting it—
to the street address of the addressee’s usual or last known place of residence; or
to the street address of any of the addressee’s usual or last known places of business; or
to any other address, if the addressee has notified the person that he or she accepts notices at the address.
A notice given by post is treated as having been given at the time it would have been delivered in the ordinary course of post.

history.history-note: Section 218(6): amended, on 1 September 2017, by section 347 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017 (2017 No 5).

prov.heading: Consent to electronic transactions

para.text: A person who gives his or her electronic address to any other person under this Act is treated as having consented to use, provide, or accept information in an electronic form for all of the purposes of this Act and Part 4 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017.
This section does not apply to the Commissioner of Inland Revenue or any employee or officer of the Inland Revenue Department.

history.history-note: Section 219(1): amended, on 1 September 2017, by section 347 of the Contract and Commercial Law Act 2017 (2017 No 5).
Section 219(2): added (with effect on 1 July 2007), on 19 December 2007, by section 95 of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).

prov.heading: Special rules about giving of product disclosure statements

para.text: Sections 217 to 219 apply to the giving or supplying of a product disclosure statement under this Act (including for the purposes of the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013) as if it were the giving of a notice.

history.history-note: Section 220: replaced, on 1 December 2014, by section 96 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.label: 220B
prov.heading: Information sharing

para.text: The Commissioner and a provider may, for the purposes of administering this Act or a scheme, communicate to each other by electronic means—
a person’s name, date of birth, and tax file number:
a person’s contact information including their address and phone number:
information relating to a person’s transfer from a KiwiSaver scheme or complying superannuation fund (an old scheme) to a new KiwiSaver scheme (a new scheme) including:
the information mentioned in paragraphs (a) and (b):
the effective date of the transfer:
details of the new scheme or old scheme such as the scheme’s registered name and number, address, and tax file number:
the date on which the person first became a member of a KiwiSaver scheme, if the old scheme is a KiwiSaver scheme:
information about claims made under section 68C of the Tax Administration Act 1994 for the person:
information relating to the administration of the estate of a person who is a deceased member of a scheme.
Nothing in subsection (1) removes an obligation imposed on a provider under this Act to communicate information.

history.history-note: Section 220B: replaced, on 2 June 2016, by section 218 of the Taxation (Transformation: First Phase Simplification and Other Measures) Act 2016 (2016 No 27).
Section 220B(1)﻿(d): inserted, on 1 April 2024, by section 171 of the Taxation (Annual Rates for 2023–24, Multinational Tax, and Remedial Matters) Act 2024 (2024 No 11).
Heading: inserted, on 1 April 2025, by section 244 of the Taxation (Annual Rates for 2024–25, Emergency Response, and Remedial Measures) Act 2025 (2025 No 9).

prov.label: 220C
prov.heading: Members may transfer QROPS accumulations to QROPS

para.text: A member may, at any time during that person’s membership of a KiwiSaver scheme, give notice to the provider of the scheme to transfer the member’s QROPS accumulation to a QROPS.
On notification by a member, the provider must, if the member has obtained the written consent of the QROPS to accept that transfer,—
transfer the member’s QROPS accumulation to the QROPS; and
give notice to the member of the amount so transferred.

history.history-note: Section 220C: inserted, on 1 April 2025, by section 244 of the Taxation (Annual Rates for 2024–25, Emergency Response, and Remedial Measures) Act 2025 (2025 No 9).

prov.label: 220D
prov.heading: Providers may transfer QROPS accumulations to QROPS

para.text: A provider of a KiwiSaver scheme may, at any time during a person’s membership of that scheme, transfer the member’s QROPS accumulation to a QROPS in accordance with this section.
The provider must obtain the written consent of—
the member to the transfer of the member’s QROPS accumulation to the QROPS; and
the QROPS to receive the transfer of the member’s QROPS accumulation.

history.history-note: Section 220D: inserted, on 1 April 2025, by section 244 of the Taxation (Annual Rates for 2024–25, Emergency Response, and Remedial Measures) Act 2025 (2025 No 9).

prov.heading: Refunds made by direct credit to bank account

para.text: A refund by the Commissioner of contribution must be made by direct credit to a bank account nominated by the person entitled to the refund.
When a person claims the refund, the person must provide to the Commissioner the particulars of a bank account in New Zealand to which a direct credit of the amount of the refund is to be made.

However, if the Commissioner is satisfied that the application of subsections (1) and (2) would result in undue hardship to a person, or is not practicable, the refund of contribution may be made by other means acceptable to the Commissioner.

In this section, bank account means an account with a bank that is a registered bank within the meaning of the Banking (Prudential Supervision) Act 1989 or a private savings bank or a credit union or a building society or the PSIS Limited.

history.history-note: Section 221(1): amended (with effect on 1 July 2007), on 19 December 2007, by section 96(1) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 221(2): amended (with effect on 1 July 2007), on 19 December 2007, by section 96(2) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 221(3): amended (with effect on 1 July 2007), on 19 December 2007, by section 96(3) of the Taxation (KiwiSaver) Act 2007 (2007 No 110).
Section 221(4): amended, on 1 July 2022, by section 300(1) of the Reserve Bank of New Zealand Act 2021 (2021 No 31).

prov.label: 221B
prov.heading: Commissioner may make certain assumptions

para.text: The Commissioner is entitled, in the absence of information to the contrary, to assume the following:
for the purposes of sections 69(1)﻿(b), 73(1)﻿(c), and 85(1)﻿(b), that an amount included in employment income information has been deducted from salary or wages:

for the purposes of section 85(2)﻿(a), if employment income information includes the date of the payday on which an amount was deducted from salary or wages, that the amount was deducted from salary or wages on that date:

for the purposes of sections 73(1)﻿(c), 85(1)﻿(b), and 95B(1)﻿(b), that an employer to whom an amount included in employment income information relates has sufficient funds available at the relevant time to be able to pay the amount to the Commissioner.

history.history-note: Section 221B: inserted, on 1 April 2020, by section 46 of the Taxation (KiwiSaver, Student Loans, and Remedial Matters) Act 2020 (2020 No 5).

prov.heading: Role of Commissioner under this Act

para.text: The Commissioner has the function of ensuring oversight of those provisions of this Act that are administered in the Inland Revenue Department under section 224.
Without limitation, this includes the functions and powers of—
requiring persons affected by this Act to verify certain matters with the Commissioner as requested by the Commissioner; and
requiring persons to give notice to the Commissioner to ensure that this Act is complied with.

prov.heading: Use of information by Commissioner obtained under this Act and other Inland Revenue Acts

para.text: Despite anything in any other Act, nothing prevents the Commissioner or any officer of the Inland Revenue Department from—
using information obtained under this Act for the purposes of carrying into effect any of the Inland Revenue Acts; or
using information obtained under any of the Inland Revenue Acts for the purposes of carrying into effect the provisions of this Act.

prov.heading: Administration of Act

para.text: Parts 1 to 3 are administered in the Inland Revenue Department.
Part 4 and Schedule 1 are administered in the department of State that, with the authority of the Prime Minister, is responsible for the administration of those provisions.

This Part is administered in the department of State that, with the authority of the Prime Minister, is responsible for the administration of those provisions, and different departments of State may be authorised to administer different provisions of this Part.

history.history-note: Section 224(1): amended, on 1 December 2014, by section 97(1) of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Section 224(2): amended, on 1 December 2014, by section 97(2) of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Fee subsidies

history.history-note: Section 225: repealed, on 1 December 2014, by section 98 of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Crown contribution: kick-start contributions

para.text: The contribution must be paid as soon as practicable after the date provided by subsection (1B) or (1C).
Unless subsection (1C) applies, the date for the purposes of subsection (1A) is the last day of the 62-day period that starts on either the date under paragraph (a) or the date under paragraph (b)—
the date under this paragraph is the earlier of—
the date, decided by the Commissioner, when the Commissioner receives the first contribution in respect of A, if A is a person to whom subpart 1 of Part 3 applies:
the date that the Commissioner is given notice or otherwise knows that A is a member of the KiwiSaver scheme:
the date under this paragraph is the date, decided by the Commissioner, when A is first a member or must first become a member as provided by section 15(1)﻿(b) or 36(1)﻿(b).

If A has transferred to their first KiwiSaver scheme from a complying superannuation fund, and A was a member of the complying superannuation fund for more than 62 days before transferring, the date for the purposes of subsection (1A) is the day on which the Commissioner is given notice that A has transferred or the day, decided by the Commissioner, that A has transferred.
The provider must use the contribution allocation for A to credit the contribution across the investment products of the KiwiSaver scheme to which A has subscribed or been allocated.
The contribution must vest in A immediately after it is paid to the provider, despite any provision to the contrary.

prov.heading: Status of Crown contribution for tax purposes

para.text: A Crown contribution paid in respect of a member of a KiwiSaver scheme under section 226 is not—
income for the purposes of the Income Tax Act 2007; or
a gift for the purposes of the Estate and Gift Duties Act 1968.

history.history-note: Section 227 heading: amended, on 1 December 2014, by section 99(1) of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).
Section 227: amended, on 1 December 2014, by section 99(2) of the Financial Markets (Repeals and Amendments) Act 2013 (2013 No 70).

prov.heading: Regulations

para.text: The Governor-General may, by Order in Council, make regulations for all or any of the following purposes:
prescribing fees payable to the Commissioner or the FMA in respect of any matter under this Act or the manner in which fees may be calculated:
prescribing the information or matters that must be included in the information packs referred to in Part 2:
prescribing provisions to be implied in the trust deed of a KiwiSaver scheme (see subsection (3)):
providing for operational matters and electronic compatibility between the Commissioner and all or any class of providers, including—
requiring the Commissioner and all or any class of providers to sign scheme provider agreements before registration of a scheme as a KiwiSaver scheme; and
providing for the updating of those agreements after registration; and
specifying the matters that may be required to be covered in all or any of those agreements:

prescribing requirements in relation to annual returns for the purposes of section 124, including the date by which the return must be provided and the 12-month period to which it must relate (by reference to annual dates):
prescribing what must be treated as reasonable efforts for the purposes of section 128:
prescribing the maximum number of persons that the Minister may appoint under section 132:
specifying information that must be contained in the instrument of appointment referred to in section 132:
providing for fees or charges that must be treated as fees for the purposes of this Act:
prescribing matters that are relevant to a determination or consideration as to whether a fee is unreasonable for the purposes of clause 2 of the KiwiSaver scheme rules or section 118:
prescribing circumstances in which the purchase of an estate in land enables a withdrawal under clause 8 of the KiwiSaver scheme rules:

prescribing circumstances for the purposes of clause 8(3)﻿(a) and (3B)﻿(a) of the KiwiSaver scheme rules or prescribing who is a qualifying person for the purpose of clause 8(3)﻿(c)﻿(ii) and (3B)﻿(c)﻿(ii) of the KiwiSaver scheme rules:
prescribing matters that may be regarded as matters from which significant financial difficulties have arisen for the purposes of clause 11 of the KiwiSaver scheme rules:
prescribing conditions that are regarded as life-shortening congenital conditions for the purposes of clause 12B of the KiwiSaver scheme rules:

recognising specific foreign superannuation schemes or classes of specific foreign superannuation schemes that are based in named countries as schemes to which funds can be transferred on permanent emigration under the provision implied by clause 14 of the KiwiSaver scheme rules:
providing for any other matters contemplated by this Act, necessary for its administration, or necessary for giving it full effect.

For the purposes of subsection (1)﻿(mb), life-shortening congenital condition means a condition that exists for a person from the date of their birth and is likely to reduce the life expectancy for persons in general with the condition below the New Zealand superannuation qualification age.
Regulations may be made under this section for the purpose of a matter dealt with in Part 4 only on the recommendation of the Minister.
A provision prescribed for the purposes of subsection (1)﻿(c) may, without limitation, do any of the following (including to supplement, or to add to, duties and powers prescribed by this Act):
specify the duties and powers of the supervisor of the scheme:
specify the duties of the manager of the scheme.
A provision implied in the trust deed under this section—
does not apply to the extent that it is inconsistent with provisions implied in the trust deed by this Act; but
applies despite anything to the contrary in the trust deed of the scheme; and
is enforceable by the manager, the supervisor, or a member of the scheme, unless the regulations provide otherwise.
Regulations under this section are secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

prov.heading: Regulations relating to mortgage diversion facility

para.text: The Governor-General may, by Order in Council made on the recommendation of the Minister of Finance, make regulations providing for mortgage diversion facilities that allow contributions in respect of a person to be withdrawn from the person’s KiwiSaver scheme and complying superannuation funds to pay amounts secured by certain mortgages relating to that person.

The Minister of Finance may make a recommendation under subsection (1) only if the Minister is satisfied that any mortgage diversion facility provided for in regulations will be consistent with the following principles:
there is no compulsion on providers to provide a mortgage diversion facility:
there is no compulsion on mortgagees to allow amounts secured by mortgages to be paid via KiwiSaver and complying superannuation fund contributions:
the mortgage diversion facility is available in relation to a person at any time after 12 months have expired since the earlier of—
the date that the Commissioner received the first contribution in respect of the person; or
the date that the relevant KiwiSaver scheme provider or complying superannuation fund provider received the first contribution in respect of that person’s membership to the relevant scheme or fund:
the mortgage diversion is available only in relation to a mortgage over the person’s principal residence (for example, the family home):

the mortgage diversion may apply for the remainder of the term of the mortgage after the diversion is made available, but only to the extent to which the mortgage continues to be over the person’s principal residence:
after the total amount secured by the mortgage is paid, ongoing contributions are not diverted from the person’s KiwiSaver scheme and complying superannuation funds:
if the person chooses to cease the mortgage diversion facility before the amounts secured by the mortgage are fully paid, the contributions are redirected towards retirement savings:
the provider, and not the Commissioner, is responsible for paying the amount diverted under the mortgage diversion facility:
the amount diverted from a person’s KiwiSaver scheme and complying superannuation funds is capped at not more than the total of—
half of the total contributions deducted for or contributed by the person, received by their KiwiSaver scheme provider; and
half of the person’s contributions to their complying superannuation funds, but limited to 4% of their annual gross base salary or wages for each complying superannuation fund:
an amount may not be diverted if it was received by the relevant provider before the provider has received from the person a request to divert amounts under the mortgage diversion facility:
employer contributions may not be diverted:
an amount that was transferred from an Australian complying superannuation scheme may not be diverted:
the facility is available for new mortgages and existing mortgages.
The regulations may specify all or any of the terms and conditions that apply to the mortgage diversion facility, including—
which types of mortgages qualify for participation in the diversion facility; and
what a scheme must do to participate in the mortgage diversion facility (for example, in relation to notification); and
how the regulations affect the trust deed (for example, whether all or any of the terms and conditions in the regulations are implied terms of the trust deed); and
what happens if the scheme decides to terminate participation in the mortgage diversion facility; and
whether payment via the mortgage diversion facility counts as payment by the mortgagor for the purpose of the terms of the mortgage; and

closing the mortgage diversion facility to new participants by specifying a date before which a member of a KiwiSaver scheme or complying superannuation fund must request his or her mortgagee to participate in the facility in respect of the member's mortgage; and
any other matters.

If a provider chooses to participate in the mortgage diversion facility, any withdrawal made in accordance with those regulations must be treated as if it were a withdrawal that is permitted under the KiwiSaver scheme rules.
Regulations under this section are secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

prov.heading: Regulations relating to default KiwiSaver providers

para.text: The Governor-General may, by Order in Council made on the recommendation of the Minister, make regulations for the following purposes:
providing for matters that may be treated by the Minister as a significant breach of an instrument of appointment for the purposes of section 137(2):
providing for procedures for the purpose of—
the Minister notifying a default KiwiSaver provider that the Minister considers the provider is in breach of a term or condition of the instrument of appointment and the possible consequences:

facilitating resolution of any dispute between the Minister and a default KiwiSaver provider about whether the KiwiSaver provider is in breach of the instrument of appointment or the consequences that should apply:
setting out any procedures that will apply before revocation of an instrument of appointment by the Minister under section 137(2):
providing for the following matters in relation to all or any class of default members of a scheme that is subject to a terminating event under section 138:

requiring those default members to be reallocated and transferred to a default KiwiSaver scheme and providing the method of determining, terms of, and procedures for the reallocation and the transfer (or providing for the Minister to require or determine any of those matters by direction to the scheme provider or providing for another person or method to determine any of those matters):
requiring the reallocation and transfer to be carried out in accordance with that method, those terms, and those procedures:

requiring the provider of a scheme that is or was a scheme provided under an instrument of appointment to do any act or thing following any terminating event under section 138 including, without limitation,—
requiring the members of the scheme to be notified of the terminating event or of any other matter and specifying time frames for notification to occur:
prescribing any advice, information, or documents that must accompany any notification given under subparagraph (i):

requiring the provider to do any act or thing or carry out any prescribed procedures to facilitate or enable the transfer of members to another KiwiSaver scheme in accordance with this Act, regulations made under paragraph (ba), or the Financial Markets Conduct Act 2013:

requiring the provider to report to any specified person or persons, or to produce to any specified person or persons, any papers, documents, records, or things in respect of the scheme, at specified times or contingent on any specified events:

requiring the provider of any new scheme to which members are or are to be transferred from a scheme that is subject to a terminating event under section 138 to do any act or thing or to carry out any prescribed procedures to facilitate or enable the transfer of members to the provider’s KiwiSaver scheme.
In this section, default members means members that were allocated to a scheme under sections 50 and 51 and are in a default investment product of a scheme.

However, regulations made under subsection (1)﻿(c)﻿(iii) may not require a provider to report to any person or produce any papers, documents, or records, after the provider’s scheme no longer has any members who became members of the scheme under section 52.
Regulations under this section are secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).

prov.label: 230A
prov.heading: Regulations relating to compulsory employer contributions

para.text: The Governor-General may, by Order in Council made on the recommendation of the Minister of Finance, make regulations prescribing a class of employees, for the purposes of the calculation of compulsory employer contributions, under section 101D(5)﻿(c)﻿(ii).

The Minister of Finance may make a recommendation under subsection (1) only if the Minister is satisfied that an employer may not prevent compulsory employer contributions increasing their employer contributions in relation to the class of employees because terms relating to their employer contributions are imposed independently of the employer and the class of employees.
Regulations under this section are secondary legislation (see Part 3 of the Legislation Act 2019 for publication requirements).
